"# Capstone Project - Part 2" 

# Model Training and Evaluation

# 1. Data Setup & Target Definition

* Feature Matrix ($X$): Consists of the continuous numerical indicators: quantity, unit_price, and discount_pct.
* Regression Label ($y_{reg}$): Defined using the continuous numeric target column profit.
* Classification Label ($y_{clf}$): A binary target column (high_profit) generated by thresholding profit at its natural sample median value ($1635.465$):$$y_{clf} = (y_{reg} > y_{reg}.\text{median}()).\text{astype}(\text{int})$$This results in perfectly balanced target benchmarks across the entire sample set ($50\%$ positive class distribution), removing any requirement for imbalance corrections like SMOTE or weighted scaling handlers.

# 2. Categorical Variables Encoding Policy

  * Nominal/Unordered Categorical Encoding: Columns without an implicit order (such as gender or product_name) are processed using one-hot encoding via pd.get_dummies(..., drop_first=True).

  * The Dummy Variable Trap: Dropping the first dummy vector prevents exact multicollinearity (where one binary indicator can be perfectly predicted by a linear combination of the remaining variables). Label encoding these attributes would implicitly introduce arbitrary numeric hierarchies (e.g., mapping Sofa = 2 and Sugar = 1 implies a false ordinal statement that a Sofa is twice the magnitude of Sugar), leading to flawed model parameter evaluations.

# 3. Leak-Free Train-Test Split and Feature Scaling
  
  * Splitting Protocol: Data is split into $80\%$ training ($400$ instances) and $20\%$ testing sets ($100$ instances) with a fixed structural constraint (random_state=42).
  * Feature Scaling: An instance of StandardScaler is initialized. It is fitted only on the training set features:
      
      # Python code
      scaler.fit(X_train)
      X_train_scaled = scaler.transform(X_train)
      X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)

# 5. Classification Model Evaluation — Logistic Regression
 
 Baseline Model Classification Performance ($C=1.0$)
 Confusion Matrix Output:$$\begin{bmatrix} 48 & 2 \\ 10 & 40 \end{bmatrix}$$
 * Accuracy: $88.00\%$
 * Macro Precision / Recall / F1: $0.89$ / $0.88$ / $0.88$
 * Area Under the Curve (AUC): $0.9416$

# Core Classification Formulas
 * Precision Notation:$$\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$$

 * Recall Notation:$$\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$$ 

* Contextual Metric Prioritization

For a business evaluating profit, Precision is often prioritized over Recall. A false positive means misclassifying a low-margin transition as high-profit, which could lead to misallocated capital or faulty strategic planning. Conversely, an AUC score of $0.9416$ indicates excellent discriminative capacity, meaning there is a $94.16\%$ probability that the classifier will rank a randomly chosen positive instance higher than a randomly chosen negative one. 

# 6. Decision-Threshold Sensitivity Matrix
Boundary Variations Table
Threshold   Precision  Recall     F1
0.30        $0.7927$  $0.9420$  $0.8609$
0.40        $0.8889$  $0.8116$  $0.8485$
0.50        $0.9464$  $0.7681$  $0.8480$
0.60        $0.9796$  $0.6957$  $0.8136$
0.70        $1.0000$  $0.5942$  $0.7455$

# Strategic Threshold Tradeoffs

* F1-Score Maximization: The operational setting that maximizes the structural $F1$-score balances out at $0.30$ ($F1 = 0.8609$).
* Optimizing for Precision: If your core objective is maximizing Precision, you should raise the decision threshold (e.g., up to $0.70$, reaching $100\%$ precision).
* The Business Cost: The cost of raising this threshold is a significant drop in Recall ($0.9420 \rightarrow 0.5942$). This means the model will miss many truly high-profit opportunities (false negatives) in order to ensure that its positive predictions are highly accurate.

# 7. Hyperparameter Regularization Experiment ($C=1.0$ vs. $C=0.01$)

Regularization Footprints Comparison

Classification Metric   Baseline Configuration     Regularized Framework 
                            (C=1.0)                        (C=0.01)
Precision                   $0.9464$                      $0.8406$
Recall                      $0.7681$                      $0.8406$
ROC AUC                     $0.9416$                      $0.9103$

# Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for AUC Performance Differences

To evaluate how consistently the baseline classifier outperforms the highly regularized framework, a localized non-parametric bootstrap resampling routine was executed over $n=500$ iterations:

 Bootstrap Simulation Results
     * Mean AUC Difference ($\overline{\Delta_{\text{AUC}}}$): $0.0313$
     * 95% Empirical Confidence Interval (CI): $[0.0039, 0.0694]$

# Statistical Significance Conclusion

Because the lower bound ($0.0039$) and upper bound ($0.0694$) of the 95% confidence interval exclude zero completely, we reject the null hypothesis of equal performance. This confirms that the baseline model ($C=1.0$) provides a statistically significant and reliable advantage in class separation capability across varying data distributions.
